title: Storage, Filesystems, Archives, and Backups · Incident Repair Bay
summary: Respond to low disk space and protect service data without confusing filesystems, mounts, archives, snapshots, and verified backups. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a storage map and restore-tested backup plan and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, a…
objectives: Relate block devices, partitions, filesystems, mount points, namespaces, and directory visibility.
Distinguish apparent size, allocated blocks, free space, inodes, deleted-open files, and per-directory usage.
Use lsblk, findmnt, mount, and filesystem identifiers to observe topology before any mount or format action.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record
steps.instruction:
debug case — Filesystem / is 92% full with 300 free inodes while a deleted-open log consumes 1.4 GiB. Build an archive and restore proof without formatting, mounting, or deleting host data.

Inspect the Block Filesystem Mount trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for relate block devices, partitions, filesystems, mount points, namespaces, and directory visibility.

content.text: For Block Filesystem Mount, distinguish a claim from evidence by locating the relevant precondition, trace or invariant, and changed input result.

hints: Name the Block Filesystem Mount precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Filesystem / is 92% full with 300 free inodes while a deleted-open log consumes 1.4 GiB. Build an archive and restore proof without formatting, mounting, or deleting host data.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A mounted device becomes a new drive letter independent of the directory tree.

stimulus.title: Block Filesystem Mount case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: A mounted device becomes a new drive letter independent of the directory tree.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Relate block devices, partitions, filesystems, mount points, namespaces, and directory visibility. Artifact under review: a storage map and restore-tested backup plan.

options.text: The record names the assumption, applies Block Filesystem Mount, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.
The record says the result looks right but supplies no quantity, trace, condition, or counterexample.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

options.text: Capture user, directory, environment, target state, and authoritative command behavior
Predict shell parsing, expansion, permissions, side effects, exit status, and safety boundary
Run the smallest simulated observation or reversible change and retain exact output
Verify changed state, failure path, rollback, and an accessible operations handoff

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair
steps.instruction: Append a safe Linux operations investigation headed "# Evidence: linux-backup-restore-proof-n6dibn" with a concrete hypothesis, one bounded simulator command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Backup Restore Proof into observable performance evidence.

Build an archive and restore proof without formatting, mounting, or deleting host data.

content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Define backup scope, consistency, retention, integrity, isolation, and restore testing as separate controls.
